# Hitbox

> Highly customizable async caching framework for Rust designed for high-performance applications.

Hitbox is an open-source cache orchestrator for Rust. Protocol-agnostic async core with first-class HTTP support via hitbox-http. Pluggable backends from in-memory to distributed solutions such as Redis. Built on Tower, works with any tokio-based service.

There are several common approaches to caching. The first is a low-level approach where you work directly with a cache instance, calling get, set, and delete methods. The second is a middleware approach where a caching layer wraps your handlers transparently. The third is function-level memoization where you annotate a function and work with it as if no caching exists. Hitbox supports the second and third approaches, keeping caching logic out of business code.

While designed for large, high-load projects, Hitbox works equally well for small ones. Configuration complexity scales with your project.

## Project structure

| Crate | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `hitbox` | Main crate with policy configuration, stale cache, and feature flags for backends |
| `hitbox-core` | Core traits (Predicate, Extractor) and types for protocol-agnostic caching |
| `hitbox-backend` | Backend trait and utilities for implementing storage backends |
| `hitbox-fn` | Function memoization with `#[cached]` macro and cache key extraction |
| `hitbox-derive` | Derive macros (`#[cached]`, `KeyExtract`, `CacheableResponse`, `CacheableRequest`) |
| `hitbox-http` | HTTP-specific predicates and extractors for request/response caching |
| `hitbox-tower` | Tower middleware integration (Cache layer) for server-side caching |
| `hitbox-configuration` | YAML/file-based configuration support |
| `hitbox-moka` | In-memory backend using Moka (lock-free, sub-microsecond) |
| `hitbox-redis` | Distributed backend using Redis (single or cluster mode) |
| `hitbox-feoxdb` | Embedded persistent backend using FeOxDB |
| `hitbox-reqwest` | Client-side caching for Reqwest via reqwest-middleware |

## Quick start — HTTP middleware (Axum / Tower)

Cargo.toml dependencies:

```toml
hitbox = "0.2"
hitbox-http = "0.2"
hitbox-moka = "0.2"
hitbox-tower = "0.2"
```

Basic usage:

```rust
use std::time::Duration;
use axum::{Router, routing::get};
use hitbox::{Config, Neutral};
use hitbox::policy::PolicyConfig;
use hitbox_http::extractors::Method as MethodExtractor;
use hitbox_http::predicates::request::Method;
use hitbox_http::predicates::response::{StatusClass, StatusCodePredicate};
use hitbox_moka::MokaBackend;
use hitbox_tower::Cache;

async fn get_users() -> &'static str { "users list" }

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let backend = MokaBackend::builder().max_entries(10_000).build();

    let config = Config::builder()
        .request_predicate(Method::new(http::Method::GET).unwrap())
        .response_predicate(Neutral::new().status_code_class(StatusClass::Success))
        .extractor(MethodExtractor::new().path("/api/users"))
        .policy(PolicyConfig::builder()
            .ttl(Duration::from_secs(60))
            .stale(Duration::from_secs(30))
            .build())
        .build();

    let cache = Cache::builder()
        .backend(backend)
        .config(config)
        .build();

    let app = Router::new()
        .route("/api/users", get(get_users).layer(cache));

    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:3000").await.unwrap();
    axum::serve(listener, app).await.unwrap();
}
```

## Quick start — Function memoization

The `hitbox-fn` crate provides function-level memoization. Annotate functions with `#[cached]`, derive `KeyExtract` on argument types to control cache key generation, and derive `CacheableResponse` on return types to control what gets stored. Build a reusable `Cache` instance with a backend and policy, then call your function via `.cache(&cache)`. Without cache configuration, `#[cached]` functions can be called directly with `.await` — transparent passthrough. Enable the `derive` feature flag for macro support.

---

## Stale cache

Each cache entry has a TTL (fresh period) and an optional stale window (grace period). During TTL, data is fresh. After TTL but within the stale window, data is stale but servable. Three policies control stale behavior:

- **Return** — serves stale data immediately without revalidation
- **Revalidate** — blocks until fresh data is fetched
- **OffloadRevalidate** — serves stale data immediately while refreshing in background (Stale-While-Revalidate pattern)

```
|── fresh ──|── stale ──|── expired ──>
0        stale_ttl      ttl
```

The OffloadManager handles background revalidation with task deduplication, configurable timeouts (none, cancel, or warn), and metrics tracking.

OffloadManager configuration:

| Option | Description | Default |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| `max_concurrent_tasks` | Limit parallel background tasks | Unlimited |
| `timeout_policy` | `None`, `Cancel(duration)`, or `Warn(duration)` | `None` |
| `deduplicate` | Prevent duplicate revalidation for same key | `true` |

```rust
let offload_config = OffloadConfig::builder()
    .max_concurrent_tasks(10)
    .timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))
    .deduplicate(true)
    .build();

let manager = OffloadManager::new(offload_config);

let policy = PolicyConfig::builder()
    .ttl(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .stale(Duration::from_secs(300))
    .stale_policy(StalePolicy::OffloadRevalidate)
    .build();

let cache = Cache::builder()
    .backend(backend)
    .config(policy)
    .offload(manager)
    .build();
```

## Dogpile prevention

When a cache entry expires or is missing, multiple simultaneous requests can trigger redundant upstream calls (thundering herd).

Hitbox uses a configurable concurrency limit per cache key:
- First N requests (N = concurrency limit) proceed to upstream
- Additional requests subscribe to a broadcast channel and wait
- When any request completes, it broadcasts the result to all waiters

With `concurrency: 1`, only one request fetches while others wait. Setting `concurrency: 2+` allows parallel fetches — the first to complete broadcasts to all waiters.

```rust
let concurrency_manager = BroadcastConcurrencyManager::<Response>::new();

let policy = PolicyConfig::builder()
    .ttl(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .concurrency(1)
    .build();

let cache = Cache::builder()
    .backend(backend)
    .config(policy)
    .concurrency_manager(concurrency_manager)
    .build();
```

## Pluggable backends

Backends store cached data via the `Backend` trait (read, write, remove). All backends support configurable serialization format (Bincode, JSON, RON, Rkyv), key format (Bitcode, UrlEncoded), compression (Gzip, Zstd), and custom naming for metrics.

| Backend | Type | Configuration |
|---------|------|---------------|
| Moka | In-memory | `max_capacity` |
| Redis | Distributed | `connection` (single or cluster mode) |
| FeOxDB | Embedded | `path` or `in_memory()` |

```rust
// Moka (in-memory)
let moka = MokaBackend::builder()
    .max_entries(10_000)
    .value_format(BincodeFormat)
    .compressor(ZstdCompressor::default())
    .build();

// Redis (distributed)
let redis = RedisBackend::builder()
    .connection(ConnectionMode::single("redis://127.0.0.1/"))
    .value_format(BincodeFormat)
    .build()?;

// FeOxDB (embedded persistent)
let feoxdb = FeOxDbBackend::builder()
    .path("/tmp/cache".into())
    .build()?;
```

Implement the `Backend` trait to add custom storage.

## Composable backends

Compose multiple backends into tiered cache hierarchies (like CPU cache levels).

| Policy Type | Options | Default |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| Read | Sequential, Race, Parallel | Sequential |
| Write | Sequential, OptimisticParallel, Race | OptimisticParallel |
| Refill | Always, Never | Never |

- **Sequential** — L1 first, then L2
- **Race** — first hit/success wins
- **Parallel** — both queried, prefer fresher (read) or succeed-if-any (write)
- **Refill Always** — populate L1 on L2 hits

Compositions can be nested for L1/L2/L3 hierarchies.

```rust
// L1 (Moka) + L2 (Redis) composition
let offload = OffloadManager::with_defaults();
let backend = moka.compose(redis, offload);

// With custom policies
let composition_policy = CompositionPolicy::new()
    .read(RaceReadPolicy::new())
    .write(SequentialWritePolicy::new())
    .refill(RefillPolicy::Always);

let backend = moka.compose_with(redis, offload, composition_policy);
```

## Serialization

| Format | Read (5KB) | Write (5KB) | Notes |
|--------|------------|-------------|-------|
| Bincode | 1.8 µs | 4.9 µs | Default, balanced |
| Rkyv | 1.1 µs | 4.9 µs | Zero-copy deserialization, best for reads |
| RON | 3.0 µs | 3.9 µs | Human-readable |
| JSON | ~300x slower | — | Debugging only |

At 1MB payload: Rkyv 166µs read / 301µs write, Bincode 197µs / 215µs, RON 308µs / 2.21ms.

## Compression

Reduce storage size via the `Compressor` trait:
- **Zstd** — excellent compression ratio with fast decompression (levels -7 to 22, default 3)
- **Gzip** — wide compatibility (levels 0-9, default 6)

Both require feature flags (`zstd`, `gzip`). Skip compression for small payloads where overhead exceeds savings.

## Observability

Built-in metrics via the `metrics` crate:
- Cache status — hit, miss, and stale counters per backend
- Latency histograms — request duration and upstream call timing
- Backend I/O — reads, writes, bytes transferred, and errors per backend
- Offload tasks — spawned, completed, deduplicated, active, and timed out

Integrates with any `metrics`-compatible exporter (Prometheus, StatsD, etc.).

## HTTP predicates

Request predicates filter incoming requests by method, path, headers, query parameters, and body. Response predicates filter upstream responses by status code, headers, and body. Both support operations like equality, existence, list matching, containment, and regex. Body predicates support size limits and JQ expressions for JSON filtering. Combine predicates with `.and()`, `.or()`, and `.not()`.

Tip: Use `Operation::Limit { bytes: N }` on response body to prevent caching large responses.

```rust
// Cache GET requests, skip if Cache-Control: no-cache
RequestMethod::new(Method::GET)
    .unwrap()
    .path("/api/authors/{id}".to_string())
    .and(
        RequestHeader::new(HeaderOperation::Contains(
            CACHE_CONTROL, "no-cache".to_string(),
        )).not()
    )

// Only cache successful responses
ResponseStatusCode::new(StatusCode::OK)
```

## HTTP extractors

Build cache keys from request components: method, path parameters (`{param}` patterns), headers, query parameters, and body. Headers and query support exact or prefix-based selection with optional regex extraction. Body extraction supports full-body hashing, JQ expressions (with `hash` function), and regex with named capture groups. Values can be transformed with hash (SHA256), lowercase, or uppercase. Extractors chain together.

```rust
MethodExtractor::new()
    .path("/v1/authors/{author_id}/books/{book_id}")
    .query("page".to_string())
    .header("Accept-Language".to_string())
```

A request to `/v1/authors/123/books/456?page=1` with `Accept-Language: en` produces a cache key with method, author_id, book_id, page, and Accept-Language components.

For sensitive headers (e.g., Authorization), use transforms:

```rust
Header::new_with(
    MethodExtractor::new(),
    NameSelector::Exact("authorization".to_string()),
    ValueExtractor::Regex(Regex::new(r"Bearer (.+)").unwrap()),
    vec![Transform::Hash],  // SHA256 hash, 16 hex chars
)
```

## Framework integration

**Servers:** Axum (full support via Tower layer). Any tower-compatible framework works since Hitbox is a standard Tower layer.

**Clients:** Hyper (full support via Tower layer), Reqwest (full support via reqwest-middleware with `hitbox-reqwest`).

## YAML configuration

Define caching setup in a configuration file — change rules at runtime without recompilation:

```yaml
backend:
  type: Moka
  max_capacity: 10000
  key:
    format: Bitcode
  value:
    format: Bincode
    compression:
      type: Zstd
      level: 3

request:
  - Method: GET
  - Path: "/api/authors/{author_id}"

response:
  - Status: Success

extractors:
  - Method:
  - Path: "/api/authors/{author_id}"
  - Query: page

policy:
  Enabled:
    ttl: 60
    stale: 300
    policy:
      stale: OffloadRevalidate
    concurrency: 1
```

---

## Benchmarks

### Throughput (50ms simulated backend)

| Metric | Without Cache | With Hitbox | Improvement |
|--------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| Throughput | 970 req/s | 167,270 req/s | 172x |
| Avg Latency | 51.6 ms | 0.295 ms | 175x |
| p99 Latency | 52.7 ms | 0.917 ms | 57x |

### Overhead

- Cache HIT: ~10-12 µs (replaces upstream latency)
- Cache MISS: ~20 µs added on top of upstream latency

| Component | Latency |
|-----------|---------|
| Predicate evaluation (method + path + 2 headers) | ~1.3 µs |
| Cache key extraction | ~5.5 µs |
| Backend read (Moka, 5KB) | ~2 µs |

### Predicate latency

| Type | Latency |
|------|---------|
| Method match | 520 ns |
| Path match | 570 ns |
| Header check | 540 ns |
| Query parameter | 1.9 µs |
| 7-predicate chain | 3.3 µs |
| JQ body predicate | 25-65 µs |

Predicates short-circuit on failure (628 ns). Place likely-to-fail predicates first.

### Backend comparison

| Backend | 1KB Read | 100KB Read | Best For |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| Moka | 1.4 µs | 58 µs | Hot path, single instance |
| FeOxDB | 51 µs | 196 µs | Persistent local cache |
| Redis | 174 µs | 328 µs | Distributed, shared cache |

### Composition overhead

| Configuration | Read (5KB) | Write (5KB) |
|---------------|------------|-------------|
| Moka only | 1.9 µs | 4.0 µs |
| Moka + Redis (2-tier) | 3.2 µs | 7.5 µs |
| 3-tier | 5.0 µs | 11.9 µs |

Each tier adds ~3-4 µs for small payloads.

### Cache key format

| Key Complexity | Bitcode | UrlEncoded | Savings |
|----------------|---------|------------|---------|
| Simple (method + path) | 32 bytes | 50 bytes | 36% |
| Complex (9 parts) | 302 bytes | 345 bytes | 12% |

---

## Blog articles

### Add Response Caching to Axum in 10 Minutes
URL: https://blog.hitbox.rs/blog/axum-caching-in-10-minutes/
Date: 2026-02-16
Tags: tutorial, axum, caching

A step-by-step guide to adding transparent HTTP response caching to an Axum application using Hitbox. Covers setting up MokaBackend with TTL, adding a Cache Tower layer to routes, response predicates to only cache 200 OK, query parameter extractors for pagination-aware cache keys, path extractors for per-resource caching, header extractors with SHA256 hashing for auth-aware caching, and Cache-Control: no-cache support for client-driven cache bypass.

Full runnable example: https://github.com/hit-box/examples/tree/main/axum-caching-in-10-minutes

### Should I Use Moka or Hitbox?
URL: https://blog.hitbox.rs/blog/moka-or-hitbox/
Date: 2026-02-28
Tags: moka, caching

Overview of the Rust caching ecosystem. Moka, Redis, and lru_cache are storage engines. Hitbox is an orchestration layer that composes them. Covers the cache storage trap (manual cache-aside boilerplate), dogpile effect under high traffic, stale-while-revalidate with split TTL phases, L1/L2 distributed caching, and how Hitbox provides all of these as built-in configurable features. Conclusion: Moka and Hitbox solve different problems and work together.
